skill.name: parallel-agents
skill.description: Guide AI assistants to split independent work across multiple agents and integrate the results safely.
skill.summary: Coordinate independent coding, testing, and review tasks across parallel AI agents.
skill.author: DMJGilbert

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged markdown fences, task examples, and ordinary words as Ruby shell execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found no executable scripts, network calls, credential access, prompt injection, or malicious intent in SKILL.md. The remaining risk is operational: parallel agent delegation can broaden changes if tasks are scoped poorly.

low_findings.title: Parallel Agent Delegation Can Broaden Change Scope
low_findings.description: The skill instructs users to launch multiple Task calls and optionally use background tasks. This is legitimate orchestration guidance, but poor scoping can cause conflicting edits or unnecessary work across agents.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly describe parallel and background agent dispatch. This is not malicious, but it creates a moderate operational concern if users ignore the skill constraints.

content.value_statement: Large coding tasks slow down when independent failures are handled one at a time. This skill shows how to split safe work across agents, define clear scopes, and combine results.
actual_capabilities: Groups independent failures or tasks by domain before delegation.
Defines agent prompts with scope, goal, constraints, and expected output.
Shows how to launch multiple Task calls in one message for parallel execution.
Explains when background tasks are useful and how to collect their results.
Provides integration steps for reviewing outputs, checking conflicts, and running tests.
limitations: It does not execute agents by itself; it provides workflow instructions.
It is not suitable for tasks that share files, state, or root causes.
It depends on the host assistant supporting Task and TaskOutput style tools.
It does not enforce permissions, test execution, or conflict resolution automatically.

use_cases.title: Triage independent test failures
use_cases.description: Split unrelated failing tests by subsystem so agents can investigate root causes at the same time.
use_cases.target_user: Software engineers maintaining large test suites

use_cases.title: Run concurrent code investigations
use_cases.description: Assign separate agents to inspect unrelated areas, then compare their findings before changing code.

use_cases.title: Overlap audit and implementation work
use_cases.description: Use background tasks for long reviews while another agent handles a bounded implementation task.
use_cases.target_user: AI coding assistant users managing multi-step projects

prompt_templates.title: Split simple test failures
prompt_templates.prompt: Use parallel-agents to group these independent test failures by file. Create one focused agent task per group with scope, goal, constraints, and expected output.

prompt_templates.title: Dispatch bounded repair agents
prompt_templates.prompt: Use parallel-agents to dispatch agents for these unrelated failures. Keep each agent limited to its listed files, and require a summary of root cause, changes, and verification.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate investigation before edits
prompt_templates.prompt: Use parallel-agents to run concurrent investigations only. Do not let agents edit files. Collect findings, identify overlap, then recommend the safest implementation order.

prompt_templates.title: Manage background and foreground work
prompt_templates.prompt: Use parallel-agents to plan a background audit and a foreground implementation task. Define how results will be collected, compared, and verified before the final response.

output_examples.input: Three unrelated test groups fail in authentication, API routing, and UI components.
output: Three agent assignments are created, one for each subsystem.
Each assignment names exact files, failure details, constraints, and required verification.
The final integration step reviews outputs, checks conflicts, and runs the full suite.

output_examples.input: A security review is long-running, but a small login form task can proceed independently.
output: The audit is assigned as a background task with a clear deliverable.
The login form work proceeds in the foreground with limited scope.
Audit results are collected before completion and reconciled with implementation changes.

best_practices: Use exact file paths, failing test names, and error messages in every agent prompt.
Add explicit constraints that prevent agents from editing shared files or unrelated modules.
Collect every agent result before finishing, then run verification across the combined changes.
anti_patterns: Do not use this workflow when failures share a likely root cause.
Do not ask multiple agents to modify the same files at the same time.
Do not send vague prompts such as asking an agent to fix all tests.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps an AI assistant divide independent work across agents and integrate the results with less conflict.

faq.question: Does it run code or shell commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation for agent orchestration and does not include executable scripts.

faq.question: When should I avoid it?
faq.answer: Avoid it when tasks share files, state, dependencies, or a likely root cause.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: The metadata lists Claude, Claude Code, and Codex support, but the workflow assumes compatible agent delegation tools.

faq.question: How should I write each agent task?
faq.answer: Include scope, goal, constraints, failure details, and the expected output for that agent.

faq.question: What is the main risk?
faq.answer: Poorly scoped parallel work can cause conflicting edits or make review harder.
